items.title: More Than Cleanliness: The Truth About OCD
items.author: vitalhealthpsychiatry
items.content:
Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD) is often misunderstood by the general public, commonly reduced to stereotypes involving excessive hand washing or cleanliness. While these symptoms may be part of OCD, the reality of this complex mental health condition goes far beyond surface-level cleanliness. OCD is a serious and often debilitating disorder that affects millions worldwide, influencing thoughts, behaviors, and daily functioning in profound ways. This article aims to shed light on the truth about OCD, debunk myths, and highlight the experiences of those living with this condition.

Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD) is an anxiety disorder characterized by persistent, unwanted thoughts (obsessions) and repetitive behaviors or mental acts (compulsions) performed to reduce the distress caused by these thoughts. These obsessions and compulsions are intrusive, time-consuming, and interfere significantly with everyday life. Unlike normal worries or habits, OCD obsessions cause intense anxiety and compulsions are performed to neutralize that anxiety, albeit temporarily.

Common Myths and Misconceptions

The most widespread misconception about OCD is that it is simply about being "too neat" or "too clean." While contamination fears and cleaning rituals are common in OCD, they represent only one subset of the disorder.

Myth 1: OCD Is Just About Cleanliness

OCD manifests in diverse ways, including: Fear of harming oneself or others.

Intrusive taboo or violent thoughts.

Excessive need for symmetry or order.

Mental compulsions like silent counting or repeating phrases.

These symptoms highlight that OCD is more than surface-level tidiness; it involves intense mental and emotional distress.: People with OCD Can Stop Their Behaviors If They Try Hard Enough OCD is not a choice or a lack of willpower. The compulsions provide temporary relief from overwhelming anxiety, making it extremely difficult to stop without professional help.: Everyone Is a Little OCD While many people may have preferences for order or cleanliness, OCD is a diagnosable mental health disorder causing significant impairment. It is much more severe than typical personality quirks.

Understanding Obsessions and Compulsions

Obsessions are intrusive, unwanted thoughts or urges that cause distress. Common obsessions include: Fear of contamination by germs or dirt.

Fear of harming oneself or loved ones.

Fear of making mistakes or being imperfect.

Intrusive sexual or violent thoughts.

Excessive concern with religious or moral issues (scrupulosity).

These thoughts are unwanted and often contradict the individual’s values, causing significant anxiety and guilt.

Compulsions are repetitive behaviors or mental acts performed to reduce the anxiety caused by obsessions. Examples include: Repeated handwashing or cleaning.

Checking locks, appliances, or safety repeatedly.

Arranging items symmetrically.

Counting or repeating words silently.

While compulsions may temporarily relieve anxiety, they often become time-consuming and disruptive.

How OCD Affects Daily Life

OCD can impact all areas of life, including work, school, relationships, and self-esteem. Many individuals with OCD spend hours each day performing compulsions or battling intrusive thoughts, which can lead to missed opportunities and social isolation. The emotional toll of OCD can include:

Intense shame or embarrassment.

Feelings of helplessness or frustration. Difficulty maintaining relationships due to misunderstandings.

Recognizing the broad impact of OCD is essential for compassion and effective support.

The exact cause of OCD remains unclear, but research suggests a combination of genetic, neurological, behavioral, cognitive, and environmental factors. Genetics: Family history can increase risk.

Brain Structure and Function: Differences in brain circuits involving decision-making and emotional regulation are implicated.

Life Stressors: Trauma or significant stress may trigger onset or worsen symptoms.

Cognitive Patterns: Certain thinking styles, such as overestimating threats or intolerance of uncertainty, contribute to OCD.

OCD diagnosis typically involves clinical evaluation by a mental health professional based on symptom history and severity.

Cognitive-Behavioral Therapy (CBT): Specifically, Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) is the gold-standard treatment. ERP involves gradual exposure to feared situations without performing compulsions, helping reduce anxiety over time.

Medications: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are commonly prescribed to help regulate brain chemistry and reduce symptoms.

Support Groups: Connecting with others living with OCD can provide validation and coping strategies.

Living with OCD: Coping Strategies

Managing OCD requires patience, commitment, and often professional guidance. Some practical tips include: Educate yourself about OCD to reduce stigma and empower self-advocacy.

Develop a structured daily routine to reduce uncertainty.

Practice mindfulness and stress reduction techniques.

Avoid avoiding feared situations; gradual exposure can build resilience.

Seek social support from trusted individuals.

Work closely with therapists and follow treatment plans consistently. Moving Beyond Stigma: Why Awareness Matters

Public awareness and understanding of OCD are crucial to improving the lives of those affected. When people recognize that OCD is a serious disorder involving distressing thoughts and compulsive behaviors—not simply "quirky habits"—they are more likely to offer empathy and encourage treatment.

Campaigns, education, and open conversations help dismantle stigma, making it easier for individuals to seek help without shame.

OCD is much more than just a preoccupation with cleanliness. It is a complex disorder involving distressing obsessions and compulsions that affect millions. Through increased awareness, proper diagnosis, and effective treatment, people living with OCD can regain control and improve their quality of life. If you or someone you know struggles with OCD symptoms, reaching out to a mental health professional can be the first step toward relief and recovery.

Keywords: Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder, OCD, OCD symptoms, OCD treatment, obsessive thoughts, compulsions, exposure and response prevention, mental health, anxiety disorder

If you'd like, I can also help with creating a resource guide or coping strategies specifically tailored for OCD!

items.title: Recuperando a soberania e a privacidade da sua comunicação pessoal e corporativa
items.author: Isadora Ribeiro
items.content:
Há cerca de 26 anos nasceu um protocolo de comunicação de código aberto, federado e com todos os princípios primordiais de liberdade e descentralização que balizaram o início da Internet.

Não... não é o SMTP - protocolo base da troca de e-mails (até porque esse é mais ancião um pouco). Foi o XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol), à época chamado também de Jabber.

Diversas soluções comerciais adotaram o XMPP para viabilizar seus produtos de mensagens instantâneas: Google Talk, WhatsApp e outros mensageiros nasceram sob as raízes do XMPP mas, ao longo do tempo, modificaram tanto o "idioma" (protocolo) que acabaram criando "dialetos" que somente usuários da mesma plataforma conseguem se comunicar.

Imagine se o Gmail enviasse e-mail apenas pro Gmail. Se o Hotmail só enviasse e-mails para Hotmail. O que seria da comunicação nos últimos 30 anos (comercialmente falando; no mínimo)?

Depois de algumas experiências com XMPP lá em 2004, esse ano eu resolvi retomar meu contato com o protocolo. Pra minha total alegria o ecossistema evoluira increvelmente: novos softwares para servidor, para cliente, bridges para outros protocolos (IRC, WhatsApp, Matrix, etc), enfim. Um admirável mundo novo.

Com toda uma mudança no cenário global de big techs, IAs, privacidade, segurança e geopolítica, e ainda "entalada" com o fato de empresas com a Meta nos colocarem num "chiqueirinho virtual" (onde só podemos conversar com pessoas de igual aplicativo) e usando cada vez mais nossos dados e hábitos para controlar que conteúdos nos serão oferecidos, resolvi tomar uma atitude.

O WhatsApp (esse é o foco, mas poderíamos estar falando de outras soluções de bigtech) acabou virando uma "tornozeleira eletrônica de bolso" pois somos praticamente obrigados a ter uma conta nesse aplicativo pois o mundo se amparou nesse meio de comunicação.

Além dessa questão, o WhatsApp é operado por uma empresa estrangeira, com políticas de uso questionáveis (recentemente foi aberta possibilidade de uso comercial por empresas de apostas, por exemplo) e sua empresa controladora tem se envolvido cada vez mais em desobediência à soberania brasileira. Ainda há a questão de que toda comunicação que passa pelos Estados Unidos é controlada e monitorada pelos seus órgãos de inteligência e segurança nacional - independente da gente pensar que a criptografia ponta a ponta do WhatsApp realmente é garantida.

Mas o princípio da Internet é a liberdade, a descentralização e, em especial, a possibilidade de escolha.

Assim nasceu o isaCloud Messenger: uma alternativa para mim, família, amigos e comunidade de ter um mensageiro instantâneo livre de bigtechs, bilionários, IAs e bets.

O que mais me apaixona sobre o isaCloud Messenger é que ele oferece:

  • usuários do isaCloud Messenger podem falar com pessoas de qualquer outro servidor que converse o mesmo "idioma" (protocolo XMPP)
  • pelo seu cliente de mensagens, é possível excluir todos os dados da sua conta, inclusive do servidor
  • opera com protocolo de comunicação de código aberto e os clientes também são de código aberto
  • as contas não são vinculadas ao seu número de celular ou qualquer outro dado pessoal: você escolhe um nome de usuário e pronto
  • você não vai receber SPAM porque seu nome de usuário não está vinculado a um número público de telefone, ou endereço de e-mail que pode ter vazado em alguma lista
  • criptografia ponta a ponta de altíssimo nível
  • criptografia de mensagens offline arquivadas para entrega futura
  • PFS (perfect forward secrecy), onde mensagens históricas só podem ser descriptografadas pelos dispositivos que trocaram essas mensagens

Se isso é bom para nossa vida pessoal, imagina para sua empresa? Poder ter um mensageiro instantâneo cujos dados são todos mantidos em sua própria infraestrutura, com sua governança e seus usuários integrados à sua base corporativa de autenticação (OIDC, por exemplo) garantindo total segurança sobre quem se comunica pelo seu mensageiro privado.

Além disso, é uma forma de se proteger com relação a questões geopolíticas e jurídicas que podem, por exemplo, banir o uso de ferramentas como Telegram, WhatsApp e Signal do país em caso de descumprimento de demandas legais.

Pessoalmente, penso que faz muito sentido recuperar a governança e soberania da comunicação das empresas brasileiras - inclusive no setor público.

A Meta, X e outros players que se cuidem: isaCloud Messenger já está operando :)

Referências sobre criptografia:

items.title: Apache Helm Chart
items.author: mahir dasare
items.content:
This Helm chart deploys an Apache HTTP Server on a Kubernetes cluster. Prerequisites

Kubernetes cluster running (local, cloud, or KIND).

kubectl installed and configured.

Helm 3 installed. Install Helm with:

curl -fsSL -o get_helm.sh https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3 chmod 700 get_helm.sh Chart Structure ├── Chart.yaml # Chart metadata

├── values.yaml # Default values for customization

└── templates/ # Kubernetes resource templates └── service.yaml Clone or navigate to the Helm chart directory.

helm package . Install the Helm chart:

helm install apache ./apache --namespace apache-namespace --create-namespace Default Configuration The chart deploys an Apache HTTP Server with the following default values (from values.yaml):

image: repository: httpd pullPolicy: IfNotPresent

service: type: ClusterIP

resources: requests: cpu: "100m" memory: "128Mi" You can customize these values by modifying values.yaml or passing them via the --set flag during installation.

Accessing the Deployment Check the status of your pods and services:

kubectl get pods -n apache-namespace kubectl get svc -n apache-namespace Forward the service port to access Apache locally:

_kubectl port-forward svc/apache 8080:80 -n apache-namespace _Open your browser and visit:

helm uninstall apache -n apache-namespace kubectl delete namespace apache-namespace

Customizing the Chart You can override default values using the --set flag. For example:

**helm install apache ./apache \ --namespace apache-namespace \ --set image.tag=2.4.53 \

--set service.type=LoadBalancer

**Alternatively, modify the values.yaml file directly.

Features Scalable: Set replicaCount to scale the number of pods.

Configurable Resources: Customize CPU/memory requests and limits.

Customizable Service: Supports ClusterIP, NodePort, or LoadBalancer service types. Verify Helm and Kubernetes versions:

_helm version kubectl versionCheck Helm release status:

_helm status apache -n apache-namespace _Inspect pod logs:

kubectl logs -n apache-namespace

items.title: Kubernetes Ingress Controller on Minikube Cluster
items.author: mahir dasare
items.content:
Steps to implement ingress: 1) Create minikube cluster as mentioned in pre-requisites :

2) Check minikube cluster status and nodes :

minikube status kubectl get nodes

3) Create one yaml file for apache deployment and service :

apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment name: apache-deployment replicas: 1 matchLabels: template: labels: spec: - name: apache ports:

apiVersion: v1 kind: Service name: apache-service selector: ports: port: 80 type: ClusterIP

4) Apply apache deployment :

kubectl apply -f apache-deployment.yaml 5) Create one more yaml file for nginx deployment and service :

apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment name: nginx-deployment replicas: 1 matchLabels: template: labels: spec: - name: nginx ports:

apiVersion: v1 kind: Service name: nginx-service selector: ports: port: 80 type: ClusterIP

6) Apply nginx deployment :

kubectl apply -f nginx-deployment.yaml 7) Enable the Ingress Controller :

minikube addons enable ingress 8) Now create an Ingress resource that routes traffic to the Apache and NGINX services based on the URL path.

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress name: apache-nginx-ingress nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: / rules:

  • host: "tws.com" http: paths:
    • path: /apache pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: apache-service port: number: 80
    • path: /nginx pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: nginx-service port: number: 80 9) Apply the Ingress resource :

kubectl apply -f ingress.yaml 10) To test the Ingress, map the hostname to the Minikube IP in your /etc/hosts file :

echo "$(minikube ip) tws.com" | sudo tee -a /etc/hosts OR Open /etc/hosts file and add your minikube ip and domain name at the last.

11) Now, test the routing :

items.title: And so it begins
items.author: Akshat Ramanathan
items.content:
Content consumption is hard. With the amount of AI generated crap out there along with the dynamic and ever evolving landscape of the web; even when I try watching videos on youtube leisurely it becomes a binging session with hard disciplined study following each video. After about a year of such binge sessions and learning new technologies and stacks I realised my YouTube watch later and favourites playlists contents have become textbooks in themselves.

So here I am, a fullstack web dev trying to annotate my thoughts as I go along surfing the web on my learning journey. From Java to PHP and from react to htmx I’ll try to cover all kinds of content to learn and discover more and more about this strange world of web and leave no stone unturned. After 2 failed attempts at microblogging, this is gonna be a new take but with a twist.

- each post contains a video Followed by the contents covered in the video and my thoughts on it. Highlighting the important parts (almost like TLDR). The goal is to make it like a diary entry of notes which I can refer back to when I think about something from any of the old videos or if I want to, a refresher to any of them.

I’m still working on the order of videos to cover. So stay tuned. The goal is to make 2 posts a week covering 2 elaborate videos. Feel free to follow me to get updates on upcoming posts and let’s begin the Dev Bytes!

items.title: 🚀 Getting Started with Solana: A Beginner-Friendly Guide
items.author: Ayush Soni
items.content:
✍️ If you're a students, builders, and blockchain beginners looking to understand Solana in plain English.

Solana is one of the fastest-growing blockchain platforms in the world — and in this article, we’ll break down , , and the that make it powerful. All explained in a clear, relatable, and builder-friendly way.

Solana is a high-performance blockchain designed for speed, scalability, and low transaction costs.

In simple terms, it’s like a super-fast, decentralized computer that anyone can use to build apps, launch tokens, create NFTs, or transfer money — without needing a central authority or third-party service.

🧠 Most blockchains are either powerful but slow, or fast but expensive. Solana is fast, affordable, and energy-efficient — making it a strong option for real-world use cases.

⚡ What Makes Solana Different from Other Blockchains?

Solana introduces a few groundbreaking technologies that set it apart. Let’s break them down:

🕰️ Proof of History (PoH) – Solana’s Secret Weapon for Speed

Most blockchains rely on validators to agree on the order of transactions, which creates delays.

A built-in clock called .

  • PoH generates a for each transaction.
  • Validators can process transactions without constantly talking to each other.

💡 Think of PoH like a timestamp machine that marks every event precisely — no arguments about what happened when.

This system helps Solana reach thousands of transactions per second with minimal delay.

🛡️ Tower BFT – Fast and Reliable Consensus

Solana uses a consensus method called Tower BFT (Byzantine Fault Tolerance).

  • Validators on which transaction block should be added next.
  • Once a vote is cast, validators are and can’t easily change it.

Combined with PoH, Tower BFT ensures that:

  • (fewer rounds of voting).
  • and hard to reverse.

🗳️ Think of it like a team vote where, once you raise your hand, you commit to that decision.

💰 Staking – How Solana Secures the Network

means locking up some SOL (Solana’s native token) to help secure the network.

  • You to a trusted validator.
  • Validators with more stake are chosen more often to process transactions.
  • In return, you .

🔐 Think of staking like putting your trust (and money) behind a good validator. More trust = more chances to lead = more rewards.

And unlike Bitcoin, this system is — it keeps Solana secure without massive electricity use.

🌍 Solana Clusters – Different Environments for Different Needs

A is a group of nodes (computers) that work together to maintain the network. Different clusters serve different purposes:

The live network with real value
Developer testing playground
Protocol updates + stress testing

🧪 Devnet is perfect for learning and experimenting — no real money required.

🪪 Wallet Addresses on Solana

Every user, smart contract, or token account on Solana has a .

  • It’s a derived from cryptographic keys.
  • Usually shown as a base-58 string (e.g., 7MNj7pL1y7XpPnN7ZeuaE4ctwg3WeufbX5o85sA91J1).
  • The signs transactions, proving ownership of that address.
  • Your is public (safe to share).
  • Your is secret (never share it!).

On Solana, everything — users, smart contracts, NFTs — has a wallet-like address.

🧾 Recap: What You’ve Learned

A fast, low-cost, scalable blockchain platform
Cryptographic clock for ordering transactions efficiently
Fast, vote-based consensus mechanism
Locking SOL to support validators and earn rewards
Different network environments for live, test, and dev use
A public key to identify users or programs on Solana

Solana is built for speed, scalability, and real-world impact. It’s beginner-friendly, eco-friendly, and growing fast.

Whether you're a college student, a junior dev, or just exploring web3 — Solana offers a powerful place to start.

In the next post, we’ll dive into how accounts work on Solana, PDAs (Program Derived Addresses), and .

🧱 Keep building, keep learning — and share this with anyone else starting their journey into Solana.

Follow for more beginner-friendly blockchain breakdowns ✨

items.title: How Template Method + Strategy Saved My Payment System (and My Sanity)
items.author: Maicon Jobim
items.content:
Real-World Applied Patterns

Picture this: it’s Friday, 6:30 PM. You’re about to leave the office when your phone rings. It’s the company’s CTO.

"The payment system is down. We’re losing money every minute. Can you take a look?"

That system you hacked together months ago to accept PayPal, then Stripe, then PagSeguro… each with their quirks, all tangled in code only you can understand — and just barely.

That night, while debugging why a PayPal change broke Stripe, you swear: .

The Nightmare Every Dev Has Lived

If you’ve ever touched payment systems, you know how it starts:

Then comes Stripe. Then PagSeguro. Then different rules for e-commerce vs subscriptions. Then marketplaces.

In a few weeks, this happens:

From Chaos to Clarity with Design Patterns

These situations led me to combine two simple but powerful patterns:

🧱 Template Method: “Define the skeleton of the operation.”

🧠 Strategy: “Plug in specific algorithms where needed.”

Most payments follow the same core steps:

  1. Prepare for processing (type-specific)
  2. Process (gateway-specific)

Let’s bring some architecture into this.

Template Method: The Unbreakable Backbone

Think of it as a structured routine — predictable, consistent:

  • Guaranteed validation & logging
  • Clear separation of custom logic
  • One place to change the core flow

Strategy: Gateway Flexibility

While Template Method gives us structure, Strategy lets us swap out behavior dynamically.

  • Easy extension for new gateways

Concrete Examples: Different Business Types

The Nightmare vs The Dream 

🔥 PROBLEMS YOU’VE PROBABLY FACED:
✅ WHAT YOU CAN ACHIEVE:

This isn’t just a story about design patterns. It’s about how clean architecture transforms your career.

When that dreaded Friday arrives, do you want to be the person — or the one who built a system that simply doesn’t break?

Template Method + Strategy won’t solve every problem — but they will solve one.

Have you lived this payment nightmare? How did you solve it? Share your story in the comments — others can learn from it.

If this helped you, share it with your team. Good knowledge is shared knowledge.

#designpatterns #cleancode #devlife #typescript #architecture

items.title: Birth of StopLang – A Useless Language With a Very Specific Purpose
items.author: sanskar arora
items.content:
So, I wanted to implement a .

Cool idea, right? Memory management, pointer wizardry, existential dread—the whole buffet.

But then came the :

“Uhhh, how the hell do I even this thing?”

You can’t really test a garbage collector in a vacuum (though that collect garbage nicely). I needed some code, some variables, some actual garbage. Something to allocate memory, throw things around, and see if the collector actually does its job—or rage quits.

Option A: Plug it into an existing language

Like a adult. Take some interpreter written in C, rip out its memory management, and bolt mine in.

But nah.

That way lies integration hell.

Do I look like someone who enjoys linker errors and segfaults from code I didn’t write? (No. No, I do not.)

Option B: Just build my own language

Like an adult.

  • 🛠️ , because I want my errors during dev time, not as jump scares during runtime.
  • ⚙️ , because:
    • C++ is friendly (a little too comfy, not enough pain).
    • Assembly is sadistic (I still like my sanity).
    • C is that spicy sweet spot of power and pain.

We're going full DIY on this one. Think of it as a language that’s just smart enough to test our GC, but dumb enough that we don’t have to spend years writing a parser.

Now, we needed a name. Something iconic. Something that tells you what this language is about.

  • It’s the . It doesn’t want to “go” anywhere.
  • It’s , and so.

It’s not fast., and it will be to build and test garbage collectors.

Bonus: It will be easier to read than Brainfuck, and more coherent than Brainrot. (Which honestly sounds like medical conditions at this point.)

This blog would turn into a research paper if I tried to cover here. So we’re breaking it down:

  1. Why StopLang exists and how I got here (hello chaos)
  2. Building the core of StopLang—basic syntax, data structures, compiler setup, maybe a sad little REPL
  3. Plugging in our garbage collectors and watching them either work… or work once and then segfault spectacularly

If you’ve read this far, bless your soul.

Thanks for bearing with the puns, the rambling, and the “what if I just wrote a language for no reason?” energy.

Stay tuned, fellow code gremlins.

More nonsense incoming soon. 😈🧹

items.title: SQL for Everyone: The Ultimate Starter Guide
items.author: Bhupesh Kumar
items.content:
A is an organized collection of data that can be easily accessed, managed, and updated. It allows software applications to store, retrieve, and manipulate data efficiently.

Key Features of a Database:

  • : Data is saved permanently (not lost after app closes).
  • : Organized using tables, collections, schemas, etc.
  • : Retrieve and filter data using query languages.
  • : Can handle large amounts of data with access control.

Common Types of Databases:

Uses tables with rows & columnsMySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite
Flexible schema, often document-based
Data stored in RAM for fast access
Stores data as nodes and edges (relationships)
  • Store user info (login/signup)
  • Manage blog posts, products, orders, etc.
  • Persist app data across sessions
  • Enable data analysis, reports, and business logic

An e-commerce app uses a database to:

  • Save user accounts and passwords
  • Store product information and inventory
  • Track orders and payments

Databases are the backbone of modern applications — allowing apps to be dynamic, data-driven, and scalable!

Choosing the right type of database is crucial for building scalable and efficient applications. Here's a breakdown of vs databases:

Table-based (rows & columns)Document, key-value, graph, or wide-column
Fixed schema (predefined)Dynamic schema (flexible)
SQL (Structured Query Language)Varies: MongoDB uses JSON-like queries
Strong support for joins & relations
Vertical (scale-up: stronger machine)Horizontal (scale-out: more machines)
Complex queries, ACID complianceLarge datasets, real-time apps, flexibility
MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, MS SQL ServerMongoDB, Firebase, CouchDB, Redis
  • Banking, finance, or systems needing strong data integrity
  • Apps requiring multi-table joins
  • Structured data with clear relationships
  • Real-time analytics or big data
  • Content management systems, IoT, or chat apps
  • Rapid development with changing requirements
{
  "_id": "1",
  "name": "Alice",
  "email": "alice@mail.com"
}

SQL (Structured Query Language) is a standard programming language used to manage and manipulate relational databases.

  • Create and modify database
  • Insert, update, and delete
  • Retrieve specific data using
  • Enforce , relationships, and data integrity
-- Create a table
CREATE TABLE users (
  id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(100),
  email VARCHAR(100)
);

-- Insert data
INSERT INTO users (id, name, email) VALUES (1, 'Alice', 'alice@mail.com');

-- Retrieve data
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = 'Alice';

A in SQL is a structured format to store in , like a spreadsheet. Each table represents a — such as users, products, or orders.

  • : Each row (record) holds data for one item.
  • : Each column represents a specific attribute (field) of the item.
  • : Uniquely identifies each row.
CREATE TABLE users (
  id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name VARCHAR(100),
  email VARCHAR(100)
);

Our First Database in SQL

Let’s create our first SQL database and table! This is your first real step into working with relational databases like MySQL, PostgreSQL, or SQLite.

Step 1: Create a Database

items.title: GeoNetwork-ui custom applications made easy
items.author: Florent Gravin
items.content:
geonetwork-ui is a powerful framework designed to help you build modern, user-friendly, fast, and responsive interfaces for your GeoNetwork catalog.

You can use it in a variety of ways:

  • Use , such as the Datahub.
  • Embed the provided directly into your site.
  • to create your own application or customize the existing ones.
  • Use the via CDN to interact with your GeoNetwork backend using JavaScript.
  • Build a using the .

This last option offers the most and , allowing you to build a custom catalog interface with minimal effort. The gnui library already includes all the logic required to interact with , along with a rich set of ready-to-use components for your application.

Let’s see how you can quickly build a custom, working application that searches your catalog and displays the results! 🚀

For this exercice, we will create a Nx workspace, which helps scaffolding all your Angular setup. itself is a monorepo powered by Nx.

npx create-nx-workspace@20.1 custom-app

and choose the following options

✔ Which stack you want to use? · angular
✔ Integrated monorepo, or standalone project? · integrated
✔ Application name · dataportal
✔ Which bundler would you like to use? · esbuild
✔ Default stylesheet format · css
✔ Do you want to Server-Side Rendering SSR ? · No
✔ Test runner to use end to end E2E tests · none
✔ Which CI provider would you like to use? · github

⚠️ the generated application in the monorepo is a application, which is not compatible with . So you have to delete the generated app and create a new app which is not standalone :

custom-app
 apps/
npx nx g @nx/angular:application esbuild apps/dataportal dataportal

Set up required dependencies

📦 requires peer dependencies to work seamlessly. You have to install stuff like :

  • for the store management
  • for translation
  • for out of the box UI components
  • and obviously, load the ! 
npx nx g ngrx-root-store gps npm 
  @ngrx/component@18.0.2 
  @angular/material@18.2.13 
  @angular/material-moment-adapter@18.2.13 
  @angular/cdk@18.2.13 
  @ngx-translate/core@15.0.0 
  @ngx-translate/http-loader@16.0.1 
  @biesbjerg/ngx-translate-extract-marker@1.0.0 
  @vendure/ngx-translate-extract@9.0.3

npm i geonetwork-ui

Set up your custom application

  • First, link the GNUI CSS at the top of apps/dataportal/src/styles.css
  • And load GNUI tailwind setup by modifying the apps/dataportal/tailwind.config.js file.
  • In , load your desired google font, which will be used for any text or title in the :

Now, we need to correctly initiate GNUI Angular modules and services. In

Create your own application now !

Now that everything is set up, you can start using GNUI components. You can take advantage of the library's , but more powerfully, you can use that interact with GNUI states (such as the search state!).

For that, you need to create a new component where you put your catalog interfaces, leveraging GNUI search components.

npx nx g @nx/angular:component OnPush apps/dataportal/src/app/search/search

In this component, inject the to setup the search state, and trigger a search on page loading to have first results.

and put the search input, and the search results like this :


        My Data Portal

Final touch, you need to initialize a search state container in your file and nest your search component inside it. All DOM elements under the gnUiSearchStateContainer directive will be linked to a specific search state.

That’s it! 🎉 You’ve now created a search state and connected two gnui components to it:

  • – provides the search input.
  • – displays the search results.

This is a very basic setup, but you're fully ready to expand it the way you like : add routing, permalink-based searches, record detail pages, news feeds, search filters, maps, and much more.

Give it a try, and feel free to comment if you get stuck or want to share what you've built! 💫

items.title: Beyond Code: What 📖The Pragmatic Programmer Taught Me in One Chapter
items.author: PatrickBastosDeveloper
items.content:
Recently, I was invited to join a Tech Book Club founded and led by @andredarcie , a Senior Backend Software Engineer who has been gathering some of the best devs he’s encountered in nearly a decade of experience in the tech world.

📚 The book for this round was the renowned "The Pragmatic Programmer" by Andrew Hunt and David Thomas — a must-read for any developer.

We discussed the first chapter for over an hour, exchanged experiences, and had a relaxed yet super productive conversation.

💭 My understanding and first impression of the book?

🧭 What’s the book’s goal? More than just teaching programming languages or frameworks, it aims to guide you toward becoming a more effective and efficient, responsible, and long-term-minded professional — someone who treats code like a true craft.

🧠 Chapter 1 – A Day in the Life This opening chapter is a call for reflection: how do you conduct yourself as a developer on a daily basis?

Here are the key takeaways:

✅ 1. You are in charge of your own career Don’t wait for your company to tell you what to learn. Stay up to date, explore new tools, and keep your radar on. A pragmatic programmer invests continuously in themselves.

✅ 2. Think before you act It may sound simple, but many developers still code on autopilot. Question, analyze, and understand the why behind every technical decision.

✅ 3. Be proactive Don’t wait for the system to break. Anticipate issues, propose solutions, automate processes, and document everything. Your attitude makes the difference.

✅ 4. Treat your code like craftsmanship Your code is your legacy. Write clearly and simply, paying attention to detail. Quality matters — especially when no one is watching.

✅ 5. Don’t live with broken windows This powerful metaphor from the book reminds us: small ignored mistakes create an atmosphere of negligence. Fix what’s wrong, even if it seems “minor.” Neglect spreads.

🚀 That’s just the beginning — and I'm already looking forward to the next chapters, which I'll certainly bring you a summary of so you can enjoy them with me!

Code with purpose. Test with strategy. Deploy with confidence.

items.title: I replaced the HTMX in the project to this module🔥
items.author: Anthony Max
items.content:
I already wrote an article on how this module can be used to replace Next.js, but it was more of a light version in some cases.

Now, finally, we get to the specifics, where the replacement is and not far-fetched. Both modules work with HTML from the server and have a similar concept, so this is definitely a good fit.

Today, we will be replacing HTMX in the project with HMPL.

Well, let's get started! 🏎️

Let's start with the simplest example we can imagine - a clicker. You could do something like this:

HTMX Clicker0
      Click!

Looks good at first glance, and most importantly, it is functional. But now, let's take an example on HMPL.js:

As we can see, we have two different approaches, where one module is completely dependent on the current DOM, and the second one is not, but is generated via JS. But, we see that in general, it is the with a common approach. Only here, the first and main problem is that in the first case we have the minimum possible set of settings for the request.

In HTMX, it was specially made so that the developer would depend minimally on js, so this is where the most serious problem of this module comes from - practically zero customization of the request.

So, yes, of course, you can send the like this:


  Click!

But still, if we want , we see that it is impossible or difficult to implement. And, once again, this is intentional, because the whole point of the module is ease of use.

Also, the second problem is that we have requests, but they are sent via , a standard that is over 25 years old. Yes, the last addition was in the early 10s, but the fact is.

In HMPL, the almost completely customizable is used, that is, consider that everything new in ECMAScript is already supported by default.

Let's use a more complex example to show how else you can replace HTMX in a project.

Login: Password: Loading...

Here we now have a form that we send to the server. The attribute includes the data as . Also, we have a request indication. Now, let's try to do the same in HMPL:

Here it is clearly visible that we can do the same as in HTMX, but there is a small but, that we can work with the , take any data from there and modify our request.

I won't keep silent about this point, but it's still worth evaluating from this side. With HTMX you will write less. Let's compare the size of applications with a simple example:

Because everything is so minimal, HTMX is the undisputed leader in terms of size, but you can see that in this example the size of the application is not very critically different.

Although, compared to others, HMPL generally shows good results in terms of dimensionality:

But, of course, if we reduce the capabilities of js, essentially doing the bare minimum, then the size of the source files will be smaller.

Of course, you can continue to use HTMX, but in this article I wanted to give an alternative to the existing option. Tasks are different and people do not always need the bare minimum in the size of the application and they are not always ready to sacrifice functionality because of this. When working with the server, you will still have to customize the request one way or another, if we are talking about large applications. But, everything depends on the task.

Thank you very much for reading this article ❤️!

What do you think about this alternative? It will be interesting to know in the comments!

items.title: Ready to Level Up Your Typing? Meet TypeWin! 🚀
items.author: Hamza
items.content:
If you’re someone who spends hours in front of a keyboard, whether coding, writing, or just messaging friends, you might be surprised at how much your typing speed affects your productivity.

The average person types anywhere between 10 to 50 WPM (words per minute), but imagine how much more you could get done with just a little improvement! 🚀 With focused daily practice, even small gains in speed and accuracy can make a big difference in your workflow.

📊 Fun Typing Stats A recent survey by onlinetyping.org (March 2025) surveyed over 56,000 volunteers, the results were fascinating:

35.51% type between 30–40 WPM

Only 1.73% type faster than 60 WPM!

73.42% type between 40–70 WPM

Only 3.4% are below 30 WPM

9.35% break the 70+ WPM mark!

✨ Why We Built TypeWin We wanted to create a typing experience that doesn’t feel like homework, it should feel like a game, a challenge, and an achievement all in one. That’s exactly what TypeWin is all about.

Now available on the Microsoft Store, TypeWin brings a fresh, exciting way to practice and improve your typing. Whether you're just getting started or chasing a new personal best, this app is built to make every session rewarding.

🚀 Features You’ll Love: ✅ Detailed Typing Stats

Know exactly where you stand, accuracy, WPM, typos, and more.

✅ Race Mode Compete against yourself or other typists around the world. It’s addictive! 🔥

✅ Global Leaderboard Climb the ranks and represent your country.

✅ Word Precision Training Pinpoint tricky words and master them.

✅ Offline & Online No internet? No problem. Your progress syncs to the cloud when you’re back online.

✅ Modern, Minimal UI Clean, beautiful, distraction-free, focus on what matters.

Currently for Windows only, but stay tuned, a web version is in the works to bring this fun experience to even more users. 🌍

items.title: Getting Started with Ansible: Automate Setups Like a Pro
items.author: Athreya aka Maneshwar
items.content:
Hi there! I'm Maneshwar. Right now, I’m building LiveAPI, a first-of-its-kind tool for helping you automatically index API endpoints across all your repositories. LiveAPI helps you discover, understand, and use APIs in large tech infrastructures with ease.

If you've ever SSHed into a bunch of servers to install the same stuff over and over, you know the pain.

Ansible solves that beautifully — it's like writing shell scripts that .

In this post, I’ll show you how to use Ansible to automate installing the across multiple remote Ubuntu servers.

is an open-source automation engine used for configuration management, application deployment, and orchestration.

Unlike Chef or Puppet, Ansible is — meaning your remote machines don’t need to install anything.

It just uses good old to get the job done.

You define you want, not to do it.

That means less bash gymnastics and more readable, version-controlled infrastructure code.

Here’s the high-level idea:

  • You write (YAML files) describing tasks like “install this package,” “copy this file,” “restart this service.”
  • You define (target machines) in an .
  • Ansible connects over SSH, interprets your playbook, and runs the tasks in order.

It’s declarative. Idempotent. Easy to debug.

Quick Primer: Ansible Syntax

An Ansible looks like this:

You can break it down like this:

Which group or machine to run this on (from inventory)
A module (built-in Ansible logic)
Argument passed to the module

Here’s a quick setup script for Ubuntu:

apt update
apt software-properties-common
add-apt-repository  ppa:ansible/ansible
apt ansible

This file tells Ansible where to go and how to log in.

Replace with your actual server IPs and SSH key paths.

Keep your secrets and project configs tidy here:

Drop your GCP service account JSON file into the same directory as the playbook.

The Playbook:

Here’s where the magic happens:

ansible-playbook  hosts.ini gcloud-install.yml

Make sure you run this as or from a root session if you're not using .

This example scratches the surface of what Ansible can do. Once you get comfy, you'll be:

  • Spinning up full environments
  • Managing Docker containers

LiveAPI helps you get all your backend APIs documented in a few minutes

With LiveAPI, you can quickly generate interactive API documentation that allows users to search and execute APIs directly from the browser.

If you’re tired of manually creating docs for your APIs, this tool might just make your life easier.

items.title: How I "Love" the Hiring Process :)
items.author: Bashar Hasan
items.content:
It's no secret—the tech job market today is rough. You've probably seen tons of posts, tweets, and videos about it. Let’s quickly break down what caused this mess:

  • COVID-19 hit, and everyone was locked inside—people and companies became more reliant on digital services.
  • Central banks started printing money to stimulate the economy, and much of that flowed into tech companies.
  • Tech companies scaled up massively, launching new products and hiring way more people than needed (I personally know folks hired in 2021 to Big Tech companies whose skill levels were honestly below average).
  • Inflation hit, thanks to all the printed money and disrupted production.
  • Central banks raised interest rates and stopped printing cash to fight inflation.
  • Tech companies began massive layoffs, cutting many of the roles they had overhired for.
  • Now, those laid-off folks—especially from big or mid-size tech—are competing again in an even tighter market.

We shouldn’t forget that the IT industry is still relatively young—only about 20 years old in terms of large-scale production. Compared to more established industries, it’s like a young person: full of strong emotions, dramatic highs, and hard falls. As a result, hiring processes, work conditions, and production methods are still dynamic and fragile, often lacking the maturity and experience found in older industries.

Open YouTube and you’ll see endless videos glamorizing tech jobs—how amazing it is to be a software engineer earning , , and “” Add to that the wave of tech influencers working at big firms who showcase the “” life of engineers, all pushed to the top by the algorithm.

Big tech companies benefit from this hype. The more people rushing into tech, the more supply there is—so salaries can stay low, even as projects get more complex (shouldn’t that raise pay instead?).

On another note, some senior developers treat others with , especially noticeable in parts of Eastern Europe. They act like you’re speaking to , , or himself.

And then there’s the fallout: , after dealing with enough developers with this “” attitude, often approach hiring with a twisted mindset. They know senior dev salaries are high, so they exploit junior and mid-level candidates instead, often with the help of senior devs.

. that seem like they’re from the 19th century? .Multi-day take-home tasks with zero feedback? . I could keep listing these all day.

Also, I should mention the filters based on , , , and . For example, if you have a passport from country , but you’re originally from country , and you’re competing with someone from for a job in — even if you’re better in every way, they’ll obviously hire the local candidate. And even if you’re half and half , that won’t necessarily help either. How I “” all this... !

Of course, there are many with supportive cultures and amazing people but those teams often fill up fast without even needing to post job ads. And let’s be honest: in toxic environments, people leave quickly. In healthy ones, they tend to stay.

How Candidates Navigate the Hiring Maze:

First of all—and most obviously—many add to their résumé (anywhere from 1 to 3 years) just to avoid being filtered out by experience requirements. As I mentioned earlier, many mid and senior devs have been laid off. Imagine competing against someone from Expedia for an entry-level job.

It’s also common for newcomers to start with jobs related to tech—like sysadmin, QA, or even call center roles—just to get their foot in the door and eventually apply for internal job postings. (Many companies post jobs internally first before going public.)

  • and look for mentorship (from “great” or actually great devs).
  • if they already have a CS bachelor's.
  • Start YouTube channels or write articles (like many on Dev.to) to build a personal brand and attract better job offers.

Oh, I almost forgot—like many others advise, starting your own startup is another path. Yes, it’s much harder than it seems, but sometimes it feels easier for my mental health than dealing with the tech job hunt. Honestly, it can feel like you’re a homeless person begging for donations from potential employers. I even realize we might reach a point where tech job seekers have to pay companies monthly just to get a chance—something I’ve seen happen in some countries based on personal experience.

Yes, as I mentioned earlier, the market is dynamic and fragile, so it will obviously change. With AI integration, more projects will emerge, increasing the need to hire people to maintain these systems and write the complex parts of the code—just like the invention of manufacturing machines created more jobs in factories. Hopefully, things will get better :)

These are just my thoughts based on personal experience—I don’t mean to hurt anyone’s feelings.Hit the reaction that match how you feel — and follow me to explore more!

You can also find me there :)

items.title: Building a Design System with Ark UI - Avatar component
items.author: Esther Adebayo
items.content:
Ark UI’s Avatar is a fully functional, unstyled component that comes with built-in accessibility features like fallbacks when the image fails to load.

In this post, we’ll walk through designing the Avatar component with Vanilla CSS and Panda CSS, including support for sizes, shapes, and visual variants that you might use across a design system.

To set up the avatar correctly, you'll need to understand its anatomy and how we name its parts. Each part includes a data-part attribute to help identify them in the DOM.

  • - The outer wrapper of the avatar
  • - The user’s profile image
  • - If the image is missing, broken, or slow to load, the fallback is shown instead

Below is the simple usage of the Avatar component:

PA

When rendered in the DOM, it looks like

EA

Each part of avatar component comes with like:

  • data-part="root" | "image" | "fallback"

This makes styling predictable for reusable design systems because you don’t need to invent class names like or worry about conflicts.

Most components come in different sizes, shapes, and variants, and if you’re not intentional with how you style, your styles can quickly get out of hand.

That’s why it helps to start .

A recipe is a structured way to define styles for a component including all its parts and variants in one place. With a recipe, you can:

  • Set up that apply to every instance of the component.
  • Add (e.g., , , ) that adjust dimensions and font size.
  • Include (e.g., , , ) for different use cases.
  • Control (e.g., , , ) to match your design system.

Essentially, recipes help you group related styles together and reuse them in a consistent way.

This keeps your components clean, your code easier to manage, and your design system more maintainable over time.

Here’s a breakdown of the available configuration options for the Avatar.

This affects both the width/height and font size (for fallback initials). Available values are:

    The variant property controls the background and text color styles for the avatar.

    • – light background with accent text
    • – bold background with white text
    • – transparent background with a border

    The shape option determines the avatar’s border-radius. Available values are:

    • – slightly rounded corners

    Let’s start by styling the Avatar using Vanilla CSS. To implement this, leverage CSS variables as much as possible to avoid repetition.

    You can find the full source code for this project onGitHub.

    These are the default, foundational styles applied to a component before any customization. Use and attributes to target the different parts.

    Add the following to your stylesheet:

    Define classes for the small and large sizes using CSS variables.

    Change the look of the Avatar with shape variants

    Lastly, define the look and feel of a component.

    Now that we’ve defined our base styles, added a few sizes, shapes, and visual style variants, let’s put them all together to see how it works in practice.

    Here’s how you can apply styles to your avatar component using classes like , , or.

    ABABAB

    Combining size, variant and shape Classes

    In addition to applying individual size classes, you can enhance the avatar's visual appearance by combining multiple class-based variants.

    This helps you get more flexibility and consistency across your UI components.

    Here’s a full example of how you’ll use the defined styles on the Avatar.

    AB

    This example renders a large, solid and fully rounded avatar.

    Recipes are a built-in concept in Panda CSS and they integrate nicely with Ark UI components. To organize styles cleanly across all parts of a component, we use a . Slot recipes come in handy when you need to apply style variations to multiple parts of a component.

    You can find the full source code for this project onGitHub.

    To define the base styles, define a slot recipe, using the function, which maps to and attributes.

    A slot recipe has 5 main properties: , , , and .

    Create an file and add the following to it

    is equivalent to listing out each part like so

    Define the recipe for the size within the key.

    Define the recipe for the shape within the key.

    Define the recipe for the visual variants within the key.

    It’s a good practice to define when setting up your recipe. This allows you to specify which variant values should be applied automatically if no specific variant is provided.

    In this case you can set the default variants as , and .

    Using the Recipe In Your Component

    To use this recipe in your component, import it:

    Next, generate the styles for each part of the component:

    This returns an object with styles for each slot: , , and .

    You then apply the styles to each component part like this:

    PA

    Combining size, variant and shape

    In addition to applying individual size classes, you can enhance the avatar's visual appearance by combining multiple class-based variants. This helps you get more flexibility and consistency across your UI components.

    PA

    This example renders a large, solid-colored, fully rounded avatar.

    Ark UI gives you the building blocks, but how you style those blocks is entirely up to you. Whether you're using Vanilla CSS or a more scalable system like Panda CSS, the important thing is setting up your variants, sizes, and styles in a

    way that’s easy to reuse and maintain as your design system grows.
